Transcrições completas de vídeos e podcasts reais de inglês — All Ears English, English Coach Chad, Luke's English Podcast e mais. Cada episódio abre a transcrição real: diálogos naturais, expressões em contexto e o vocabulário usado de verdade na conversa.

and went ow. And we all laughed. And

9:08

then the next year a new kid did the

9:10

same thing. [laughter]

9:12

It was always someone new

9:14

every year, like a tradition. Also, we

9:17

had a tuck shop, a little window where

9:19

you could buy sweets during break. I

9:21

used to spend all my pocket money there

9:23

on Fridays.

9:25

All of it.

9:26

Every single penny gone by Friday lunch.

9:28

The weekend was just waiting.

9:32

Word number four, pocket money.

9:35

Pocket money. The small amount of money

9:37

that parents give children. I used to

9:39

get pocket money every week. I saved my

9:42

pocket money for months to buy a toy. He

9:45

always spent his pocket money on sweets

9:47

like someone we know.

9:48

I don't know who you're talking about.

9:51

[laughter]

9:52

I used to save my pocket money. I was

9:55

very careful with it. I had a little

9:57

ceramic pig, a piggy bank, and I used to

10:00

put my coins in it every week.

10:01

What were you saving for?

10:03

A camera. A small camera for kids. I saw

10:06

it in a shop window and I just had to

10:08

have it. I saved for about four months

10:11

and when I finally had enough, my dad

10:13

took me to the shop and I bought it

10:15

myself. I carried the bag home like I

10:18

had just bought a house.

10:20

That is the best feeling when you buy

10:22

something with your own money for the

10:24

first time.

10:25

It really is. I took photos of

10:29

everything for a month. The garden, the

10:31

cat, my shoes, everything.

10:34

[laughter]

10:35

Word number five, school trip.

10:37

A school trip. When a whole class goes

10:41

somewhere together for the day. A

10:43

museum, a farm, a park, somewhere

10:47

interesting.

10:48

We had a school trip to the science

10:50

museum. I remember our school trip to

10:53

the farm. I used to love school trips

10:56

because you didn't have lessons.

10:58

[laughter]

10:58

That was the main reason, wasn't it?

11:01

Mostly. Yes. Okay. So, speaking of

11:03

school trips, this is the story. The one

11:06

from the beginning.

11:07

Oh, the sports day story.

11:10

Not a school trip. It was sports day.

11:13

Okay. So, every year our school had a

11:15

sports day. Parents came, teachers came,

11:18

everyone watched. I was seven and I was

11:21

in the running race, the 50 meter sprint

11:24

and

11:25

I was at the start line. I was very

11:28

focused, very serious. The teacher said,

11:30

"Ready." I was ready. The teacher said,

11:33

"Set." I was set. And then she said,

11:36

"Go." And I ran. I ran as fast as I have

11:40

ever run in my life.

11:41

And [sighs]

11:43

the wrong way.

11:46

[gasps and laughter]

11:47

I ran the wrong direction. Completely

11:49

the wrong way. All the other kids went

11:52

that way and I went that way. By the

11:55

time I understood what happened, I was

11:57

just standing there alone and all the

12:00

parents were looking at me.

12:03

Tom,

12:04

I still don't know how it happened. I

12:07

just ran the wrong way. [laughter]

12:10

[gasps]

12:11

What did you do?

12:13

I turned around and finished the race. I

12:16

came last by quite a long time, but I

12:19

finished.

12:21

[laughter]

12:22

That is amazing. I love that you

12:24

finished.

12:25

I finished. That's the important thing.

12:28

Okay, so now you know the story. Go back

Tom's Sports Day Story: What Really Happened

12:31

to your comment. Was your guess right?

12:33

Write it below. We are going to read

12:35

every single comment.

12:38

Please be kind.

12:39

Okay, let's go through everything we

12:41

learned today. Quick summary.

12:44

Grammar used to for things that happened

12:46

regularly in the past. I used to play

12:49

outside every day. She used to wake up

12:52

early. They used to walk to school

12:54

together.

12:55

And the negative is didn't used to. I

12:59

didn't used to like vegetables.

13:01

Questions. Did you used to

13:04

vocabulary word number one, grow up? I

13:08

grew up in a small town.

13:10

Word number two, best friend. My best

13:13

friend and I used to talk all night.

13:16

Word number three, playground. We used

13:18

to play on the playground every break.

13:21

Word number four, pocket money. I used

13:24

to save my pocket money for weeks.

13:27

Word number five, school trip. I used to

13:30

love school trips because there were no

13:32

lessons.

13:34

[laughter]

13:34

Word number six, sports day. Our school

13:38

sports day was every June.

Lesson Summary & Speaking Practice with "Used To"

13:40

I remember mine very well.

13:43

[laughter]

13:44

We know, Tom. We all know now. Okay,

13:47

here is your question for today. And we

13:51

really, really want you to answer this

13:53

one.

13:53

Tell us one memory from your childhood.

13:56

It can be funny, happy, embarrassing,

13:59

anything. Use the words from today if

14:01

you can. Use used to if you can.

14:04

For example, when I was a child, I used

14:07

to play football with my friends every

14:09

day. Or my best friend and I used to

14:12

build blanket forts in the living room.

14:14

Write it in the comments. We read them.

14:17

We reply. We love hearing about your

14:20

lives.

14:20

And if you want to practice more, try

14:23

writing three sentences about your

14:25

childhood with used to. It is really

14:27

good practice. Okay, that is everything

14:30

for today. Thank you so much for being

14:33

here. Every single one of you,

14:35

53,000 of you and more every day. We are

14:39

so grateful.

14:40

If you are not subscribed yet, please

14:42

hit that button now. We upload a new

14:45

lesson every single day. So there is

14:47

always something new waiting for you

14:49

here.

14:49

And come back tomorrow. We will be here.

14:52

Same time, new lesson, new story. Maybe

14:55

one of mine won't be embarrassing next

14:57

time. [laughter]

14:59

Maybe.

15:00

Maybe.

15:01

See you tomorrow, Daily English Talk

15:03

family. Keep practicing, keep going, and

15:06

keep speaking English every day. Bye.

15:08

Bye.

English SPEAKING Practice - Let's say it together!

0:00

All right, let's practice speaking English. We got a little text here. Let's see what it's about.

0:06

Let's see what this is all about. Last winter, something strange happened to me late one night.

0:13

Last winter, something strange happened to me late one night.

0:19

All right, feel free to shadow me. Say it along with me. Okay.

0:24

I was home alone because my partner was away for work and I'd decided to stay in for the evening.

0:34

I was home alone because my partner was away for work and I'd decided to stay in for the evening.

0:43

It was a cold, quiet night and the house felt unusually still.

0:50

It was a cold, quiet night and the house felt unusually still.

0:57

It was already pretty late when I decided to go to bed around 11 or maybe closer to midnight.

1:07

It was already pretty late when I decided to go to bed around 11 or maybe closer to midnight.

1:17

I'd turned off most of the lights and was walking upstairs thinking about my day.

1:27

I'd turned off most of the lights and was walking upstairs thinking about my day.

1:36

Outside the street was silent. No people, no cars passing by.

1:43

Outside the street was silent. No people, no cars passing by.

1:50

As I reached the top of the stairs, I heard a sound. As I reached the top of the stairs,

1:58

I heard a sound. At first, it was very soft and unclear, like a light tapping or a whisper.

2:09

At first, it was very soft and unclear, like a light tapping or a whisper.

2:17

I stopped and listened carefully, trying to figure out where it was coming from.

2:24

I stopped and listened carefully, trying to figure out where it was coming from.

2:33

The sound seemed to be moving, which immediately made me feel uneasy.

2:40

The sound seemed to be moving, which immediately made me feel uneasy.

2:48

A few seconds later, I heard it again. This time, it was louder and slower, almost like footsteps.

3:00

A few seconds later, I heard it again. This time, it was louder and slower, almost like footsteps.

3:10

It seemed to be coming from the right from right outside [laughter] near the front door.

3:18

It seemed to be coming from right outside near the front door.

3:25

My heart started pounding and I suddenly became very aware

3:30

that I was completely alone in the house. My heart started pounding and I suddenly became very aware

3:40

that I was completely alone in the house. I stood there for a moment trying to stay calm,

3:49

telling myself there was probably a simple explanation. I stood there for a moment,

3:58

trying to stay calm, trying telling myself there was probably a simple explanation.

4:06

Maybe someone was just walking past the house. Or maybe it was an animal moving out around outside.

4:17

Maybe someone was just walking past the house. Or maybe it was an animal moving around outside.

4:26

Still, I couldn't shake the uneasy feeling. Still, I couldn't shake the uneasy feeling.

4:36

I couldn't shake. That means to get rid of. If you're If you shake something off,

4:41

you get rid of it. I couldn't shake it. Couldn't get rid of it. Make it go away.

4:50

I decided to go downstairs and see what was going on.

4:56

I decided to go downstairs and see what was going on.

5:02

I walked down slowly trying not to make any noise. I walked down slowly trying not to make any noise.

5:13

When I got to the front door, I didn't open it right away.

5:18

When I got to the front door, I didn't open it right away.

5:23

Instead, I went to the window and looked outside. Instead, I went to the window and looked outside.

5:34

The street looked perfectly normal. No people, no cars, nothing moving.

5:41

The street looked perfectly normal. No people, no cars, nothing moving.

5:48

The street lights were on and everything was quiet. The street lights were on and

5:55

everything was quiet. I started to feel a little silly and told myself I'd probably imagined it.

6:06

I started to feel a little silly and told myself I'd probably imagined it.

6:15

But just as I was about to walk away, I heard the noise again.

6:22

But just as I was about to walk away, I heard the noise again.

6:28

This time it was much clearer and it really did sound like footsteps right in front of the door.

6:39

This time it was much clearer and it really did sound like footsteps right in front of the door.

6:50

My stomach tightened and I stood there listening. My stomach tightened and I stood there listening.

7:01

After a few seconds, I took a deep breath and slowly opened the door.

7:09

After a few seconds, I took a deep breath and slowly opened the door. But just kidding. Did I

7:19

scare you? Did I make you jump? Outside, [snorts] sitting calmly on the step was a large black cat.

7:32

Outside, sitting calmly on the step was a large black cat.

7:39

It was wearing a collar with a small bell that jingled whenever it moved.

7:46

It was wearing a collar with a small bell that jingled whenever it moved.

7:54

The cat looked at me for a moment, then got up and walked off into the darkness.

8:02

The cat looked at me for a moment, then got up and walked off into the darkness.

8:10

I closed the door, locked it, and quietly laughed at myself.

8:15

I closed the door, locked it, and quietly laughed at myself. I felt relieved, but also a little

8:25

foolish for getting so scared. I felt relieved, but also a little foolish for getting so scared.

8:35

Still, before I went to bed, I checked the door twice before finally falling asleep.

8:43

Still, before I went to bed, I checked the door twice before finally falling asleep.

All right, that was our fun little story. Let's go ahead and shadow him now. I'm going to say it

8:57

nonstop, nice, fast, natural pace. See if you can keep up with me. You can just listen

9:05

or you can shadow me. Here we go. Last winter, something strange happened to me late one night.

9:14

I was home alone because my partner was away for work and I decided to stay in for the evening.

9:22

I was a cold it was a cold, quiet night and the house felt unusually still.

9:30

It was already pretty late when I decided to go to bed around 11 or maybe closer to midnight.

9:39

I'd turned off most of the lights and was walking upstairs, thinking about my day.

9:46

Outside, the street was silent. No people, no cars passing by. As I reached the top of the stairs,

9:54

I heard a sound. At first, it was very soft and unclear, like a light tapping or a whisper.

10:04

I stopped and listened carefully, trying to figure out where it was coming from.

10:11

The sound seemed to be moving, which immediately made me feel uneasy.

10:18

A few seconds later, I heard it again. This time, it was louder and slower, almost like footsteps.

10:29

It seemed to be coming from right outside near the front door. My heart started pounding

10:37

and I suddenly became very aware that I was completely alone in the house. I stood

10:44

there for a moment trying to stay calm, telling myself there was probably a simple explanation.

10:54

Maybe someone was just walking past the house. Or maybe it was an animal moving around outside.

11:03

Still, I couldn't shake the uneasy feeling. I decided to go downstairs and see what was

11:12

going on. I walked down slowly, trying not to make any noise. When I got to the front door,

11:22

I didn't open it right away. Instead, I went to the window and looked outside.

11:30

The street looked perfectly normal. No people, no cars, nothing moving.

11:37

The street lights were on and everything was quiet. I started to feel a little silly and

11:44

told myself I'd probably imagined it. But just as I was about to walk away, I heard the noise again.

11:54

This time it was much clearer, and it really did sound like footsteps right in front of the door.

12:03

My stomach tightened, and I stood there listening. After a few seconds, I took a deep breath

12:11

and slowly opened the door. Outside, sitting calmly on the step, was a large black cat.

12:20

It was wearing a collar with a small bell that jingled whenever it moved.

12:27

The cat looked at me for a moment, then got up and walked off into the darkness.

12:35

I closed the door, locked it, and quietly laughed at myself.

12:41

I felt relieved, but also a little foolish for getting so scared.

12:46

Still, before I went to bed, I checked the door twice before finally falling asleep.

12:55

Boom. Good workout. Way to go. Thank you for practicing with me again today. Keep

Listen, Learn, Repeat & Shadow English109 linhas

Listen, Learn, Repeat & Shadow English

0:00

Are you ready to listen to the instruction manual? I prepared a little instruction

0:05

manual for you today. This is going to be mostly a listening exercise, but you can

0:10

you can learn, repeat, and shadow with me. Do the best you can. I'm going to go fast.

0:15

I'm going to go fast, and I'm not going to pause much. So, here we go. You ready?

0:22

Do you know why so many people study English for years but still struggle to speak with confidence?

0:30

It's not because they're lazy. It's not because they're weak.

0:35

It's because they spend all their time training their mind but not their mouth.

0:42

Think about it. You memorize grammar rules. You read books. You get good scores on exams.

0:50

But when it's time to actually speak, your tongue feels heavy.

0:56

Your mind goes blank and your confidence quietly disappears. Here's the simple truth.

1:04

To speak English naturally and confidently, you have to train your mouth, not just your mind.

1:13

Imagine this. You didn't learn to ride a bicycle by reading an instruction manual. You didn't

1:21

learn to ride a bicycle by reading an instruction manual, right? You learned by getting on the bike,

1:28

losing your balance, falling, trying again, and slowly moving forward.

1:35

Speaking English works the same way. It's not about how much English you know,

1:42

it's about how much English your mouth has practiced saying.

1:48

0:00

All right. Are you ready to win the morning? Let's do it. Now, we're going to listen to English.

7 segundos

13 segundos

Now, the repeating part, I'm not going to pause a lot, but I will pause a little so you can repeat

20 segundos

chunks of speech that you want to remember. Shadowing. If you're an expert English practicer,

0:27

27 segundos

try to match my pace. Try to say it with me. All right, here we go. When you're morning, before the world wakes,

0:39

39 segundos

before the world wakes up, when you're morning, I want you to picture something simple and real.

0:48

48 segundos

The alarm goes off. It's still dark. The room is quiet. No messages, no noise, no demands.

1:01

1 minuto e 1 segundo

In that moment, you are not reacting to life. You are deciding how you will live it.

1:13

1 minuto e 13 segundos

That first hour of your day carries more power than most people understand.

1:22

1 minuto e 22 segundos

It shapes your attitude. It affects your focus. It even influences how you handle money and how much progress you make.

1:39

1 minuto e 39 segundos

If you lose your morning, you spend the rest of the day trying to catch up. If you win your morning, you spend the rest of your day leading.

1:55

1 minuto e 55 segundos

Most people wake up and immediately hand their mind over to the world. Hand over to that's a

2:05

2 minutos e 5 segundos

phrasal verb. I just highlighted it. To to give to yield control to hand it over.

2:13

2 minutos e 13 segundos

They grab their phone. They scroll. They read messages. They read

2:23

2 minutos e 23 segundos

I put it in t past tense, didn't I? They read messages that create stress. Oh, they look at other people's lives before they look at their own goals.

2:43

2 minutos e 43 segundos

Their heart rate goes up.

2:48

2 minutos e 48 segundos

Their thoughts become scattered. And before their feet touch the floor, they already feel behind.

3:01

3 minutos e 1 segundo

That is not how a disciplined person lives. Winning your morning starts the night before.

3:13

3 minutos e 13 segundos

It starts with a decision. You decide what time you will rise. You decide what you will do in that first hour.

3:28

3 minutos e 28 segundos

You prepare your clothes. You prepare your workspace. You remove excuses before they appear.

3:42

3 minutos e 42 segundos

Discipline is not about force. It's about preparation. When the alarm rings, your mind will offer you comfort.

3:56

3 minutos e 56 segundos

It will say stay in bed. It will say you deserve more rest.

4:05

4 minutos e 5 segundos

It will say one more day will not matter.

4:11

4 minutos e 11 segundos

That voice is normal. Every human has it. The difference between those who grow and those who stay stuck is simple.

4:26

4 minutos e 26 segundos

One group listens to comfort. The other group listens to commitment.

4:38

4 minutos e 38 segundos

Get up when you said you would. Sit up in your bed. Feel the floor under your feet.

4:50

4 minutos e 50 segundos

Take a slow breath. Tell yourself clearly why you are awake.

4:59

4 minutos e 59 segundos

Not in a loud, dramatic way, but in a steady and calm way.

5:08

5 minutos e 8 segundos

Remind yourself that this day matters. Remind yourself that your future is built in hours like this.

5:21

5 minutos e 21 segundos

Go wash your face. Feel the cold water. It wakes your body. It sends a signal that you are alert.

5:36

5 minutos e 36 segundos

Drink a glass of water. Your body has been resting for hours. It needs fuel.

5:45

5 minutos e 45 segundos

These are small actions but small actions done daily create identity.

5:55

5 minutos e 55 segundos

You start seeing yourself as someone who is serious about life. Now protect that first 30 minutes of your morning like it's valuable because it is.

6:13

6 minutos e 13 segundos

Do not check your phone. Do not open social media. Sit in silence for a few minutes.

6:22

6 minutos e 22 segundos

Notice your thoughts. Are you anxious? Are they negative? Are they hopeful?

6:31

6 minutos e 31 segundos

You cannot manage your mindset if you never observe it. Write down three things you are grateful for.

6:44

6 minutos e 44 segundos

simple things, your health, your family, the opportunity to work.

6:52

6 minutos e 52 segundos

Gratitude steadies your emotions. It shifts you from pressure to purpose.

7:05

7 minutos e 5 segundos

A calm mind makes better decisions, especially about money and time.

7:14

7 minutos e 14 segundos

After gratitude, write down your top priorities for the day. Not 20 tasks, but three to five important actions.

7:30

7 minutos e 30 segundos

When you limit your focus, you increase your power. you stop reacting to everything and start moving towards something specific.

7:46

7 minutos e 46 segundos

Your morning is also the best time to strengthen your body.

7:54

7 minutos e 54 segundos

Move for at least 20 minutes. Walk, stretch, do push-ups. Feel your muscles working.

8:07

8 minutos e 7 segundos

Feel your breathing deepen. Physical movement improves your mental clarity.

8:18

8 minutos e 18 segundos

When your body is strong, your mind becomes more stable. Energy creates confidence.

8:27

8 minutos e 27 segundos

During this early hour, your mind is clear. It has not been filled with noise.

8:38

8 minutos e 38 segundos

Use that clarity to learn. Read a few pages of a book that improves your thinking.

8:51

8 minutos e 51 segundos

Listen to something that challenges you. Not entertainment. Education.

8:59

8 minutos e 59 segundos

Your income and your progress grow when your knowledge grows.

9:07

9 minutos e 7 segundos

The quiet morning is the perfect classroom. Winning your morning is not about waking up at an extreme hour.

9:20

9 minutos e 20 segundos

It's about waking up with intention. If you rise before the world, you are not rushed.

9:33

9 minutos e 33 segundos

You are not reacting. You are building. Consider how your mood changes when you are rushed.

9:46

9 minutos e 46 segundos

When you wake up late, you hurry. You skip breakfast. You snap at people.

9:55

9 minutos e 55 segundos

You make quick choices. You spend money on convenience because you feel stressed.

10:05

10 minutos e 5 segundos

That stress drains your momentum. Now consider a different morning. You wake up early.

10:16

10 minutos e 16 segundos

You sit quietly. You move your body. You plan your day. You step into the world calm and prepared.

10:28

10 minutos e 28 segundos

Your voice is steady. Your decisions are thoughtful. You say no to things that do not match your goals.

10:41

10 minutos e 41 segundos

You you that calm focus follows you into your work, into your finances, into your relationships.

10:53

10 minutos e 53 segundos

The first win of the day builds confidence. It tells your brain that you keep promises to yourself.

11:07

11 minutos e 7 segundos

Trust. Self-rust is powerful. When you trust yourself, you take bigger steps.

11:17

11 minutos e 17 segundos

You invest wisely. You stick to your plans.

11:22

11 minutos e 22 segundos

You continue when results are slow. Some people say they are not morning people.

11:32

11 minutos e 32 segundos

I understand that feeling. Habits are strong, but habits can be changed.

11:41

11 minutos e 41 segundos

Start 15 minutes earlier than usual. Keep it simple.

11:48

11 minutos e 48 segundos

Consistency matters more than intensity. The early morning also gives you emotional space.

12:02

12 minutos e 2 segundos

During the day, people need you. Your job demands attention.

12:11

12 minutos e 11 segundos

Messages arrive. Problems appear.

12:16

12 minutos e 16 segundos

If you do not create quiet time for yourself, your own goals get pushed aside

12:27

12 minutos e 27 segundos

and years pass quickly when you live only in reaction.

12:35

12 minutos e 35 segundos

Winning your morning is an act of self-respect.

12:41

12 minutos e 41 segundos

It says your growth matters. It says your peace matters. There will be mornings when you feel tired.

12:54

12 minutos e 54 segundos

There will be days when motivation is low. On those days, do something small. But do not skip the routine.

13:08

13 minutos e 8 segundos

Every five minutes of reflection is better than none. Even five minutes of reflection is better than none.

13:20

13 minutos e 20 segundos

Momentum is fragile. Protect it. Fragile. Make sure I pronounce that right. Momentum

13:30

13 minutos e 30 segundos

is fragile. It could be broken. Protect it. Over time, this early discipline shapes your identity.

13:42

13 minutos e 42 segundos

You no longer see yourself as someone who struggles to get started.

13:49

13 minutos e 49 segundos

You see yourself as someone who leads your own life. That shift in identity changes everything.

14:02

14 minutos e 2 segundos

Your morning sets the tone for how you think about money.

14:09

14 minutos e 9 segundos

When you plan your day early, you are more aware of how you spend.

14:18

14 minutos e 18 segundos

You track your expenses. You think about long-term goals. You are less likely to make emotional purchases because your mind is steady.

14:34

14 minutos e 34 segundos

Your morning sets the tone for your mindset.

14:40

14 minutos e 40 segundos

If you begin with gratitude and focus, you respond to challenges with patience.

14:49

14 minutos e 49 segundos

You do not panic at every obstacle. You understand that setbacks are part of growth, but they do not define you.

15:04

15 minutos e 4 segundos

15:22

15 minutos e 22 segundos

15:35

15 minutos e 35 segundos

There is something powerful about seeing the sun rise while you are already moving forward.

15:47

15 minutos e 47 segundos

The light slowly fills the room. The day begins and you are not behind.

15:57

15 minutos e 57 segundos

You are ahead in mindset, ahead in preparation, ahead in discipline.

16:06

16 minutos e 6 segundos

People often look for big breakthroughs. They want dramatic change.

16:17

16 minutos e 17 segundos

16:30

16 minutos e 30 segundos

It is built on small choices no one applauds. Win your morning before the world wakes. And you will notice something.

16:44

16 minutos e 44 segundos

You feel more in control. You feel less reactive.

16:51

16 minutos e 51 segundos

You feel stronger inside. That inner strength carries you through the rest of the day.

17:01

17 minutos e 1 segundo

17:15

17 minutos e 15 segundos

Do not waste that chance by handing your mind to distractions.

17:24

17 minutos e 24 segundos

Rise with purpose. Sit with clarity. Move with energy.

17:34

17 minutos e 34 segundos

Plan with focus. Learn with intention.

17:39

17 minutos e 39 segundos

Step into the day prepared. When you control your morning, you begin to control your life.

17:52

17 minutos e 52 segundos

Improve English Practice_ Real-life Sentences A1 to A2.437 linhas

Improve English Practice: Real-life Sentences A1 to A2.

0:00

Welcome to English Evolution Step One,

0:04

your first step toward confident,

0:07

practical English. In this lesson,

0:10

you'll learn how one idea can grow from

0:14

a short sentence to a fluent expression.

0:18

From A1 to A2, we'll guide you through

0:23

100 real life sentence pairs that build

0:27

your voice, your rhythm, and your

0:30

confidence.

0:33

I am ready.

0:37

I am ready.

0:41

I am ready for the exam.

0:46

I am ready for the exam.

0:52

She is afraid.

0:56

She is afraid.

1:00

1:05

She is afraid of the dark.

1:16

1:21

1:25

He is late for school.

1:37

We are friends.

1:41

1:46

We are good friends at school.

1:57

3:49

16:42

He meets his teacher after class.

16:47

You've just taken your first step from

16:51

A1 to A2.

16:54

From short sentences to fluent

16:57

expression,

16:59

from simple words to real connection.

17:04

Your English is growing and your voice

17:07

is getting stronger. So, don't stop

17:10

here.

17:12

Subscribe to English Evolution Step 1

17:17

for more poetic practical lessons.

17:21

Share this video with someone who's

17:23

ready to grow with you.

17:26

Comment your favorite sentence below and

17:29

let's keep learning together.

17:32

[snorts] This is English evolution step

17:36

one. Your journey has begun. Let's keep

17:40

moving step by step.

0:03

idea can grow from a short sentence to a

fluent expression.

Improve English Practice_ Transportation in English.607 linhas

Improve English Practice: Transportation in English.

0:00

Let's learn about land transport. The vehicles we use on roads every day.

0:09

Car.

0:19

I go to work by car.

0:27

Bus. Bus.

0:34

We wait for the bus every morning.

0:39

We wait for the bus every morning.

0:47

Taxi. Taxi.

0:53

She takes a taxi to the hospital.

0:59

She takes a taxi to the hospital.

1:13

1:19

They travel to the city by train.

motorbike. Motorbike

1:33

1:39

He rides his motorbike to school.

1:47

1:53

1:59

I ride my bicycle in the park.

2:06

2:19

The truck carries food to the shop.

2:39

We move our things in a van.

2:46

2:59

3:19

3:40

garage. Garage.

4:39

5:00

5:18

5:39

We wait on the platform.

6:18

schedule schedule.

6:39

The bus schedule is on the wall. Great job. Now you know the different

6:47

ways we travel on land. Next, let's explore air transport. The

6:53

fastest way to travel long distances.

6:59

7:07

We fly to Paris by airplane.

7:13

We fly to Paris by airplane.

7:20

7:33

7:40

7:53

8:13

8:33

8:53

boarding pass. Boarding pass.

luggage is heavy. Her luggage is heavy.

The airline is very good. Well done. You've learned the words we

use when flying in the sky. Now, let's look at water transport. The

12:06

boats and ships that move on rivers and seas.

life jacket. Life jacket.

destination. Destination

us travel safely. Now, let's learn travel actions. The

33:16

trip.

34:03

34:08

They go on holiday by plane. Great job today. You've taken another

34:15

step forward in your English journey. Remember, practice a little every day.

34:21

Small steps make big progress. Congratulations. You've completed step

34:27

one of English evolution. Now you can talk about transport, travel, and the

34:33

essentials of moving around in daily life. Keep practicing these words and sentences. They will make your English

34:40

stronger every day. Remember, every step you take brings you closer to fluency.

34:47

Stay confident, stay curious, and keep smiling with your English. See you in

34:53

the next lesson.

Improve English: Action Verbs With Pronouns in English A1.

0:00

English verbs training with personal pronouns in action.

0:06

Drive. Drive.

0:13

I drive to work. I drive to work.

0:20

We drive together. We drive together.

0:28

You drive well. You drive well.

0:36

They drive fast. They drive fast.

0:43

He drives a taxi. He drives a taxi.

0:51

She drives every day. She drives every day.

1:01

Ride ride.

1:08

I ride my bike. I ride my bike.

1:17

We ride horses. We ride horses.

1:26

You ride the bus. You ride the bus.

1:35

They ride scooters. They ride scooters.

1:44

He rides a motorcycle. He rides a motorcycle.

1:53

She rides with her friend. She rides with her friend.

2:01

Fly fly.

2:08

I fly to Paris. I fly to Paris.

2:16

We fly in a plane. We fly in a plane.

2:26

You fly tomorrow. You fly tomorrow.

2:35

They fly often. They fly often.

2:44

He flies for work. He flies for work.

2:52

She flies with her family. She flies with her family.

3:01

Wait. Wait.

3:08

I wait for the bus. I wait for the bus.

3:17

We wait in line. We wait in line.

3:26

You wait here. You wait here.

3:34

They wait outside. They wait outside.

3:43

He waits for his friend. He waits for his friend.

3:52

She waits at the door. She waits at the door.

4:01

Leave. Leave.

4:07

I leave at 8. I leave at 8.

4:16

We leave together. We leave together.

4:26

You leave now. You leave now.

4:34

They leave early. They leave early.

4:43

He leaves the house. He leaves the house.

4:52

She leaves her bag. She leaves her bag.

5:01

Arrive. Arrive.

5:07

I arrive on time. I arrive on time.

5:17

We arrive at school.

5:22

We arrive at school. You arrive late.

5:31

You arrive late. They arrive tomorrow.

5:41

They arrive tomorrow. He arrives at work.

5:48

He arrives at work. She arrives with a smile.

5:56

She arrives with a smile.

6:01

Travel. travel.

6:09

I travel a lot. I travel a lot.

6:18

We travel by train. We travel by train.

6:28

You travel for fun. You travel for fun.

6:36

They travel every summer. They travel every summer.

6:44

He travels for business. He travels for business.

6:52

She travels with her sister. She travels with her sister.

7:01

Turn. Turn.

7:08

I turn left. I turn left.

7:17

We turn the page. We turn the page.

7:25

You turn the light on. You turn the light on.

7:34

They turn around. They turn around.

7:43

He turns the key. He turns the key.

7:52

She turns slowly. She turns slowly.

8:01

Cross cross.

8:08

I cross the street. I cross the street.

8:17

We cross the bridge. We cross the bridge.

8:26

You cross here. You cross here.

8:35

They cross together. They cross together.

8:44

He crosses the road. He crosses the road.

8:52

She crosses with care. She crosses with care.

9:01

Love. Love.

9:07

I love music. I love music.

9:15

We love our family. We love our family.

9:24

You love chocolate. You love chocolate.

9:33

They love to dance. They love to dance.

9:42

He loves his dog. He loves his dog.

9:51

She loves flowers. She loves flowers.

10:01

Hate. Hate.

10:07

I hate noise. I hate noise.

10:16

We hate waiting. We hate waiting.

10:25

You hate cold weather. You hate cold weather.

10:34

They hate traffic. They hate traffic.

10:43

He hates onions. He hates onions.

10:52

She hates being late. She hates being late.

11:02

Smile. Smile.

11:08

I smile at you. I smile at you.

11:17

We smile in photos. We smile in photos.

11:26

You smile every day.

11:32

You smile every day.

11:37

They smile together. They smile together.

11:46

He smiles at the baby. He smiles at the baby.

11:53

She smiles with joy. She smiles with joy.

12:02

Cry. Cry.

12:08

I cry sometimes. I cry sometimes.

12:17

We cry during movies. We cry during movies.

12:27

You cry easily. You cry easily.

12:35

They cry at night. They cry at night.

12:44

He cries when he's sad. He cries when he's sad.

12:52

She cries quietly. She cries quietly.

13:01

laugh. Laugh.

13:08

I laugh with friends. I laugh with friends.

13:17

We laugh a lot. We laugh a lot.

13:27

You laugh loudly. You laugh loudly.

13:36

They laugh at jokes. They laugh at jokes.

13:44

He laughs at the story. He laughs at the story.

13:52

She laughs with her sister. She laughs with her sister.

14:01

Hug. Hug.

14:08

I hug my mom. I hug my mom.

14:16

We hug each other. We hug each other.

14:24

You hug your friends. You hug your friend.

14:33

They hug before leaving. They hug before leaving.

14:42

He hugs his son. He hugs his son.

14:51

She hugs her grandma. She hugs her grandma.

15:01

Kiss kiss.

15:08

I kiss my baby. I kiss my baby.

15:16

We kiss goodbye. We kiss goodbye.

15:25

You kiss your child. You kiss your child.

15:34

They kiss on the cheek. They kiss on the cheek.

15:43

He kisses her hand. He kisses her hand.

15:52

She kisses the baby. She kisses the baby.

16:02

Meet me.

16:09

I meet new people. I meet new people.

16:19

We meet at the cafe.

16:24

We meet at the cafe. You meet your teacher.

16:33

You meet your teacher. They meet every week.

16:43

They meet every week. He meets his friend.

16:50

He meets his friend. She meets her cousin.

16:57

She meets her cousin. Call

17:09

I call my brother. I call my brother.

17:18

We call the doctor. We call the doctor.

17:27

You call now. You call now.

17:36

They call every day. They call every day.

17:45

He calls his boss. He calls his boss.

17:53

She calls her mom. She calls her mom.

18:02

Visit. Visit.

18:09

I visit my aunt. I visit my aunt.

18:18

We visit the museum. We visit the museum.

18:28

You visit often. You visit often.

18:37

They visit their friends. They visit their friends.

18:45

He visits his hometown. He visits his hometown.

18:54

She visits her uncle. She visits her uncle.

19:02

Phone. phone.

19:08

I phone my dad. I phone my dad.

19:18

We phone the office. We phone the office.

19:27

You phone your friend. You phone your friend.

19:35

They phone every evening. They phone every evening.

19:45

He phones his teacher. He phones his teacher.

19:53

She phones her sister. She phones her sister.

20:02

Text. Text.

20:08

I text my friend. I text my friend.

20:18

We text each other. We text each other.

20:27

You text quickly. You text quickly.

20:37

They text all day. They text all day.

20:46

He texts his mom. He texts his mom.

20:53

She texts her cousin. She texts her cousin.

21:02

Email email.

21:09

I email my teacher. I email my teacher.

21:18

We email the school. We email the school.

21:28

You email your boss. You email your boss.

21:38

They email the company. They email the company.

21:47

He emails his friend. He emails his friend.

21:54

She emails her manager. She emails her manager.

22:02

Send. Send.

22:10

I send a message. I send a message.

22:19

We send gifts. We send gifts.

22:28

You send the file. You send the file.

22:37

They send money. They send money.

22:46

He sends a photo. He sends a photo.

22:54

She sends a letter. She sends a letter.

23:03

Search. Search.

23:10

I search online. I search online.

23:20

We search for answers. We search for answers.

23:29

You search well. You search well.

23:38

They search the house. They search the house.

23:45

He searches the room. He searches the room.

23:53

She searches for her keys. She searches for her keys.

24:02

Click. Click.

24:09

I click the link. I click the link.

24:18

We click together. We click together.

24:27

You click too fast. You click too fast.

24:36

They click on the video. They click on the video.

24:45

He clicks the button. He clicks the button.

24:54

She clicks the icon. She clicks the icon.

25:02

Type. type.

25:08

I type my name. I type my name.

25:18

We type the report. We type the report.

25:28

You type quickly. You type quickly.

25:37

They type on laptops.

25:43

They type on laptops. He types the email.

25:50

He types the email. She types her story.

25:58

She types her story. Post.

26:05

Post. I post a photo.

26:14

I post a photo. We post online.

26:23

We post online. You post every day.

26:32

You post every day. They post comments.

26:41

They post comments. He posts news.

26:49

He posts news. She posts her art.

26:58

She posts her art.

27:03

Share. Share.

27:11

I share my food. I share my food.

27:20

We share ideas. We share ideas.

27:30

You share your story.

27:35

You share your story. They share everything.

27:43

They share everything. He shares his book.

27:51

He shares his book. She shares her smile.

27:57

She shares her smile.

28:03

Watch. Watch.

28:09

I watch a video. I watch a video.

28:18

We watch together. We watch together.

28:27

You watch the show. You watch the show.

28:35

They watch cartoons. They watch cartoons.

28:45

He watches the clip. He watches the clip.

28:53

She watches quietly. She watches quietly.

29:03

House. House.

29:10

I live in a house.

29:15

I live in a house.

29:20

We clean the house. We clean the house.

29:30

You like this house. You like this house.

29:39

They build houses. They build houses.

29:47

He paints the house. He paints the house.

29:54

She enters the house. She enters the house.

30:02

Kitchen. Kitchen.

30:08

I cook in the kitchen. I cook in the kitchen.

30:17

We clean the kitchen.

30:22

We clean the kitchen. You love your kitchen.

30:31

You love your kitchen. They eat in the kitchen.

30:39

They eat in the kitchen. He walks into the kitchen.

30:49

He walks into the kitchen. She sings in the kitchen.

30:57

She sings in the kitchen.

31:03

bed. Bed

31:10

I sleep in my bed. I sleep in my bed.

31:18

We make the bed. We make the bed.

31:27

You jump on the bed. You jump on the bed.

31:36

They lie on the bed.

31:42

They lie on the bed. He rests in bed.

31:50

He rests in bed. She reads in bed.

31:58

She reads in bed. Chair.

32:07

Chair. I sit on the chair.

32:16

I sit on the chair. We move the chairs.

32:24

We move the chairs.

32:29

You fix the chair. You fix the chair.

32:38

They carry chairs. They carry chairs.

32:47

He paints the chair. He paints the chair.

32:54

She chooses a chair. She chooses a chair.

33:03

Table. Table.

33:10

I clean the table. I clean the table.

33:18

We eat at the table. We eat at the table.

33:27

You set the table. You set the table.

33:36

They move the table.

33:42

They move the table. He builds a table.

33:49

He builds a table. She decorates the table.

33:57

She decorates the table.

34:02

Door. Door.

34:09

I open the door.

34:15

I open the door.

34:20

We close the door.

34:25

We close the door. You knock on the door.

34:35

You knock on the door. They fix the door.

34:44

They fix the door. He locks the door.

34:50

He locks the door. She paints the door.

34:58

She paints the door.

35:03

Cheap. Cheap.

35:10

I buy cheap shoes. I buy cheap shoes.

35:18

We like cheap food. We like cheap food.

35:27

You find cheap clothes. You find cheap clothes.

35:37

They sell cheap books. They sell cheap books.

35:46

He chooses the cheap one. He chooses the cheap one.

35:55

She wants something cheap. She wants something cheap.

36:03

Expensive. Expensive.

36:10

I avoid expensive things. I avoid expensive things.

36:19

We skip expensive places. We skip expensive places.

36:28

You buy expensive gifts. You buy expensive gifts.

36:37

They sell expensive phones. They sell expensive phones.

36:45

He wears expensive shoes. He wears expensive shoes.

36:54

She likes expensive perfume. She likes expensive perfume.

37:03

phone. Phone.

37:10

I use my phone. I use my phone.

37:19

We charge the phone. We charge the phone.

37:28

You drop your phone. You drop your phone.

37:36

They call on the phone. They call on the phone.

37:45

He checks his phone. He checks his phone.

37:54

She answers the phone. She answers the phone.

38:02

Video. Video.

38:09

I watch a video. I watch a video.

38:17

We record a video. We record a video.

38:26

You send the video. You send the video.

38:35

They post the video. They post the video.

38:44

He edits the video. He edits the video.

38:53

She shares the video. She shares the video.

39:03

Friend.

39:11

I call my friend. I call my friend.

39:19

We meet our friend. We meet our friend.

39:27

You help your friend. You help your friend.

39:36

They visit their friend. They visit their friend.

39:45

He hugs his friend. He hugs his friend.

39:54

She talks to her friend. She talks to her friend.

40:03

Family. Family.

40:11

I love my family. I love my family.

40:19

We live with family. We live with family.

40:27

You call your family. You call your family.

40:36

They support their family. They support their family.

40:46

He misses his family. He misses his family.

40:55

She cooks for her family. She cooks for her family.

41:04

Happy. Happy.

41:11

I feel happy today. I feel happy today.

41:18

We are happy together. We are happy together.

41:28

You look happy. You look happy.

41:35

They seem happy. They seem happy.

41:44

He is happy now. He is happy now.

41:52

She stays happy always. She stays happy always.

42:03

Sad. Sad.

42:08

I feel sad sometimes. I feel sad sometimes.

42:18

We are sad today. We are sad today.

42:26

You look sad. You look sad.

42:34

They feel sad, too. They feel sad, too.

42:43

He is sad about the news. He is sad about the news.

42:53

She seems sad and quiet.

42:58

She seems sad and quiet.

43:03

Thank you. Thank you. You've just taken one more step toward real English, real

43:09

confidence, and real connection. From morning greetings to daily routines,

43:14

every sentence brings you closer to fluency that feels natural and true.

43:20

Keep listening, keep practicing, keep feeling. Your journey is just beginning,

43:26

and every word you speak is a seed of growth. And don't forget to subscribe and like English Evolution Step One for

43:33

more practical English lessons.

Personal Pronouns In English Grammar for (A1–C2).921 linhas

Personal Pronouns In English Grammar for (A1–C2).

0:00

What if one word could change how you see yourself, how you speak, how you

0:06

connect, how you belong? These aren't just pronouns. Their power,

0:13

their presence, their poetry. From beginner to mastery. This is your

0:20

journey through personal pronouns. Six levels, five types, one voice. Yours.

0:28

Let's begin. Personal pronouns in English. Here are six

0:33

levels. A1, A2, B1, B2, C1, C2. We will

0:42

dive into subject pronouns, object pronouns, possessive pronouns,

0:48

possessive adjectives, and reflexive pronouns in the easiest order, step by

0:55

step. Girl, friends,

1:01

couple man, woman,

1:07

computer, cat. Welcome to the beginning. A1, beginner.

1:17

Here we meet the words that help us speak about ourselves and others.

1:22

Simple, clear, friendly. Let's learn how to say I, we, you, they,

1:32

he, she, and, it. Let's look at these examples with pronouns.

1:40

Subject pronouns. I am tired.

1:46

I am tired.

1:53

We are ready. We are ready.

2:06

You are nice.

2:12

You are nice.

2:19

They are here.

2:25

They are here.

2:32

He is tall.

2:38

He is tall.

2:45

She is happy.

2:51

She is happy.

2:59

It is cold.

3:05

It is cold.

3:10

Object pronouns. Help me.

3:18

Help me.

3:24

Call us.

3:29

Call us.

3:36

I see you.

3:43

I see you.

3:50

Watch them.

3:56

Watch them.

4:04

Meet him.

4:10

Meet him.

4:16

Ask her.

4:21

Ask her.

4:29

Move it.

4:34

Move it. Possessive

4:41

adjectives. My book.

4:49

his phone.

its name.

6:21

6:32

6:44

reflexive pronouns. I hurt myself.

8:03

8:16

9:13

You've just taken your first step. Now you can talk about people, things, and

9:19

yourself with confidence. Let's keep going. The journey has only begun. A2

9:26

high beginner. Now we go a little further. You already

9:32

know the basics. Now let's use them in real life. Shopping. talking, helping,

9:40

sharing. Let's make your English more natural and useful.

9:46

Subject pronouns. I go shopping on Fridays.

9:54

I go shopping on Fridays.

10:02

We play music together.

10:07

We play music together.

10:13

You speak clearly.

10:19

They walk to school.

They walk to school.

10:46

10:59

She likes dancing.

It looks old.

11:26

11:38

Well done. You've added more detail, more rhythm, more life to your words.

17:35

Next, we'll explore how to express ideas with even more clarity.

17:41

B1 intermediate. Welcome to the middle ground. You're no

17:46

longer just learning, you're communicating. Let's use personal pronouns in emails,

17:53

meetings, and everyday stories. This is where your English starts to feel like

17:59

your own. Subject pronouns. I usually check my

18:05

emails first. I usually check my emails first.

He travels often for work.

20:57

25:43

meaning is clearer. Let's keep building with confidence and creativity.

25:51

B2 upper intermediate. Now we speak with nuance. You're ready to express

25:58

opinions, make decisions, and explain yourself. Personal pronouns help you

26:04

sound natural, professional, and precise. Let's refine your voice.

26:10

26:19

33:53

classroom, and the world. Let's move toward elegance and depth.

C1 advanced. Welcome to the world of expression.

34:13

tone, and subtle meaning. Let's speak with grace and intention.

Subject pronouns. I often reflect on past decisions.

words fail. I find myself drawn to silence when words fail.

Luke_s English Podcast2 episódios
(x) 872. The Birthday Party (Learn English with a Short Story)736 linhas

872. The Birthday Party (Learn English with a Short Story)

0:00

Hello, welcome back to Luke's English Podcast. In this episode, I'm going to tell you a story and then use it to help you learn English, okay?

0:07

And I'm gonna do this in three sections. First, I'll just tell the story to you.

0:12

And you can use that as a sort of little listening exercise or listening test, see if you understand everything in the story, and especially things like how the characters feel and

0:22

exactly what happened. It's a fairly short story, this one. I mean, it's a very short story, really.

0:28

It's 300 words, about 300 words long. So it'll just take a couple of minutes for me to read the story to you. And then after that,

0:35

I will just explain what happened in the story in my own words. And then I'll go through the story again, line by line, and I'll explain vocabulary for you

0:46

so you can learn various little phrases, words, and other descriptive language from this story,

0:53

okay? So the story that we're looking at here is called The Birthday Party, and it was written by

0:59

Catherine Brush, who was an American newspaper columnist, a short story writer, a novelist. This

1:08

particular story was published in 1946. But like all good writing, it still remains fresh today.

1:17

It feels like it could have been written yesterday, really. So although it was written a few decades ago, it still feels really sort

1:25

of fresh and new. So I'm gonna start in just a second, but first of all, just one question for you to consider before you listen to this, okay? My question is about people-watching.

1:35

Do you know what people-watching is? This is when you're in a public place, for example, in a restaurant, and you kind of watch the other people around you and sort of,

1:47

I don't know, imagine what their lives are like, observing other people. I mean, it's a bit nosy, right, to do this, to look at other people and sort of spy on them,

1:57

but I think we all do this. So do you ever people-watch, okay, when you're in a public place

2:03

like a restaurant or in a queue for something? So observing people, watching their behaviour,

2:09

watching their interactions with each other, and then imagining what kinds of people they are,

2:14

what their lives are like, what their relationships are like. It might also include eavesdropping, which means actually listening to other

2:22

people's conversations, but not necessarily. So people-watching, just watching people, watching

2:27

strangers that you might see in public places. Do you ever do this? If you're out in public,

2:32

do you ever discreetly watch the other people around you and try to imagine what their lives

2:38

are like? For example, if you're in a restaurant, do you subtly, while you're eating your food,

2:43

do you subtly, without being noticed, have a look at the other people on the other tables, try to imagine who they are, what's going on in their lives? And have you ever observed something

2:55

amusing or something that made you feel a little uncomfortable or embarrassed? Like

3:01

maybe you got an insight into someone's personal relationship or family life, and it actually made

3:08

you feel even a little bit uncomfortable or embarrassed or awkward or even sad. So do you ever people-watch, and have you ever noticed anything sort of interesting

3:18

when doing that? That's just something to think about before we start the story. And in fact,

3:24

let's now start the story. All you need to do here is just listen first. Just listen and try and follow the main events. How do the characters

3:32

feel? I'll explain in my own words in a moment, and then we'll go through all the vocabulary and stuff and see what we can learn. So here we go.

Story starts

3:43

This is The Birthday Party by Catherine Brush. They were a couple in their late 30s,

3:51

and they looked unmistakably married. They sat on the banquette opposite us

3:57

in a little narrow restaurant having dinner. The man had a round, self-satisfied face with glasses on it. The woman was fadingly pretty

4:09

in a big hat. There was nothing conspicuous about them, nothing particularly noticeable.

4:18

Until the end of their meal, when it suddenly became obvious that this was an occasion. In fact,

4:27

it was the husband's birthday, and the wife had planned a little surprise for him. It arrived in

4:37

the form of a small but glossy birthday cake with one pink candle burning in the centre.

4:45

The head waiter brought it in and placed it before the husband, and meanwhile, the violin

4:51

and piano orchestra played happy birthday to you. And the wife beamed with shy pride over her little

5:00

surprise, and such few people as there were in the restaurant tried to help out with a pattering

5:06

of applause. It became clear at once that help was needed because the husband was not pleased.

5:18

Instead, he was hotly embarrassed and indignant at his wife for embarrassing him. You looked

5:27

at him and you saw this, and you thought, oh, now, don't be like that. But he was like that.

5:37

And as soon as the little cake had been deposited on the table and the orchestra had finished the birthday piece and the general attention had shifted from the man and the woman,

5:50

I saw him say something to her under his breath, some punishing thing,

5:56

quick and curt and unkind. I couldn't bear to look at the woman then,

6:05

so I stared at my plate and waited for quite a long time. Not long enough, though.

6:14

She was still crying when I finally glanced over there again,

6:21

crying quietly and heartbrokenly and hopelessly all to herself under the gay

6:28

big brim of her best hat. So that's the story. I think that's very good writing.

Story summary & comments

6:39

I think that's a really, really well-written little story, a perfect little portrait of these two people and a little interaction, which seems to tell

6:49

you so much about what's going on between them. And it's sad. It's definitely sad.

6:55

It's almost heartbreaking, really. Really good writing, though. But what happened? Okay, so the story begins.

7:05

It's in a restaurant, okay? The writer says that she saw this couple. They were probably

7:14

in their late thirties and they were definitely married. She could tell just by looking at them.

7:20

And they were sitting just on the other side of the restaurant, just opposite them at a table,

7:25

having dinner. She described the two of them. She said that the man had a round face,

7:31

glasses, and he had a sort of self-satisfied look on his face. The woman was fadingly pretty. Like she kind of used to be pretty or she sort of,

7:42

her beauty is fading or maybe getting less as she gets older,

7:50

maybe. And they just looked like an ordinary couple, really.

7:55

They weren't bringing too much attention to themselves, except for the fact that the woman was wearing a nice big hat with a wide brim. And the narrator of the story realises

8:08

that this is not just an ordinary dinner, but in fact, it's a special occasion. In fact,

8:13

it's the husband's birthday and the wife has planned a special little surprise for him.

8:19

And she seems to be kind of a bit shy about it and quite proud of the fact that she's organised this little special thing for his birthday. And what is the

8:29

special thing? It's a little birthday cake, a cute little birthday cake, a red glossy cake with a single pink candle in the middle of it. And she's obviously asked

8:39

the head waiter of the restaurant to bring the cake over and the orchestra, just like

8:46

a couple of instruments playing in the corner of the restaurant, they start playing happy birthday. The staff come over, they're probably singing happy birthday and the cake is

8:55

brought down. The wife seems very sort of proud of this moment. And the people in the restaurants all give a little clap.

9:05

They all applaud. And it seems like a very sweet moment, but what the narrator notices

9:13

is that when all the people have gone away and when no one's looking at them,

9:19

she notices that the husband is not happy with this at all. Now, why? Why would you not be happy with that situation? It's not really clear.

9:28

I don't know either really, because I'd be delighted if my wife had organised a sweet little thing like that in a nice little restaurant,

9:37

but this man is not happy about it. In fact, he's very embarrassed,

9:43

which seems to tell you something about him. Like he's probably got quite a high opinion of himself.

9:48

He probably takes himself very seriously. And the idea that like a little cake and people in the restaurant singing happy birthday to him and everyone looking at him is actually

9:58

something that he's ashamed of and something that made him feel very embarrassed. And he's unhappy.

10:04

And in fact, he's angry about it. And they're trying not to show this to

10:09

everyone in the restaurant, but he's really unhappy with her. And when no one's looking,

10:15

except for the narrator of the story who notices this, the husband said something to his wife and it's really something very nasty.

10:24

He says it quietly so other people can't hear it, but he says a nasty comment to her, which is really sad. And she's obviously very,

10:34

very upset and she's heartbroken. The writer of the story can't really bear to watch.

10:43

So she looks at her plate and stuff for quite a long time. But when she does have a little look again, she sees that the woman is still crying.

10:54

And under the brim of her lovely, happy, big hat, she's still crying.

11:00

So it's a really sad little story and a story about observing a relationship,

11:08

observing a marriage, which perhaps is not that happy, not the happiest marriage, or at least,

11:16

I don't know, it's a sensitive situation, or at least the woman, she's obviously crying because her husband's unhappy, but also probably, I imagine in a situation like that,

11:27

when you've planned something and it goes so wrong, it probably not only makes you feel

11:33

unhappy in that moment, but I imagine she's feeling very sad and depressed and it makes you wonder about the state of their marriage and whether this woman is trapped in a sort of

11:43

a loveless marriage, despite the fact that she's trying her best, she's making efforts. And it's

11:52

also quite heartbreaking because it makes you realise that a broken heart or unhappiness,

12:00

loneliness, sometimes these things can be hidden. They're not obvious.

12:06

They can be hidden behind appearances, which could otherwise be very glamorous or very sort

12:13

of smart. In this case, a lovely hat and a nice special occasion in a nice restaurant,

12:21

but behind this facade, there's a sort of a deep loneliness and the sense that people in

12:30

marriages can actually be very lonely. It can be one of the loneliest things.

12:36

Not always, of course, often marriage is a beautiful and happy situation, but in some cases,

12:43

it can be actually a very sad situation. And loneliness is something that is often most deeply

12:51

felt when you're in the company of other people, especially when you feel trapped in that situation

12:58

and when you're having to hide your feelings because other people can see you. And this story

13:05

like perfectly captures all of those things in an efficient way, only in about 300 words.

13:12

So it's really, really well-written. Now let's go through the story again. And this time I'm going to look at specific words and phrases,

Vocabulary explanations

13:22

right? This is where I'm gonna teach you some vocabulary by using the story. So here we go again, the birthday party by Catherine Brush vocabulary. And I've highlighted

13:33

some vocab here for us to look at, okay? If you're listening to the audio version of this episode,

13:42

you can find the script for this. I'll put a link to it on the episode page. So you can actually check the text and stuff

13:48

like that. If you're looking at the video version, you'll be able to see the text on the screen. Another thing actually to mention is pronunciation.

13:56

So just earlier there in this episode, I read through the story line by line. Now

14:01

what you could do, if you want to practise your pronunciation, you could actually try to repeat those lines after me. Okay, try to repeat the story after me with exactly the same delivery.

14:12

So just try and copy the way I do it and then maybe practise it again on your own. Practise trying to deliver the story with the right pausing, the right emphasis and so on,

14:24

okay? And another exercise you could do would be to try to retell the story in your own words,

14:30

right? Try to tell the story again, but sort of spontaneously in your own words without

14:38

actually reading it from the text. So all of those things can be good ways to practise.

14:45

But anyway, let's have a look now at some vocabulary. So the first line. They were a couple in their late thirties and they looked unmistakably married.

14:54

So the first thing is just this phrase, in their late thirties. So, you know, you can be in your thirties, which means you're either 30,

15:02

you're between the ages of 30 and 39. So you can be in your teens, that's when you're a teenager.

15:09

You can be in your twenties, in your thirties, in your forties, in your fifties and so on. But also

15:14

you can say, you could be in your early thirties or your mid thirties or your late thirties,

15:20

okay? In this case, the couple were in their late thirties, okay? And they looked

15:25

unmistakably married. So it's fairly easy to understand they looked married. It looked like they were married. So they looked married, but they looked unmistakably married,

15:36

okay? They were obviously married. You couldn't mistake them for anything else, right? So if

15:44

something is unmistakable, it just means it's really not easily confused for something else.

15:50

Like when you see it, you immediately identify it as that thing. There's no mistaking the fact that these people were married. They were unmistakably married,

15:59

right? So unmistakable is the adjective, unmistakably in this case is the adverb.

16:05

when she said they were unmistakably married? She's probably referring to certain behaviour. Now, when a couple are new, when they've just got together,

How to Learn English with my podcast 🎧 [959]

0:00

Hello, welcome back to Luke's English Podcast. In this episode, I'm going to tell you a story and then use it to help you learn English, okay?

0:07

And I'm gonna do this in three sections. First, I'll just tell the story to you.

0:12

And you can use that as a sort of little listening exercise or listening test, see if you understand everything in the story, and especially things like how the characters feel and

0:22

exactly what happened. It's a fairly short story, this one. I mean, it's a very short story, really.

0:28

It's 300 words, about 300 words long. So it'll just take a couple of minutes for me to read the story to you. And then after that,

0:35

I will just explain what happened in the story in my own words. And then I'll go through the story again, line by line, and I'll explain vocabulary for you

0:46

so you can learn various little phrases, words, and other descriptive language from this story,

0:53

okay? So the story that we're looking at here is called The Birthday Party, and it was written by

0:59

Catherine Brush, who was an American newspaper columnist, a short story writer, a novelist. This

1:08

particular story was published in 1946. But like all good writing, it still remains fresh today.

and they looked unmistakably married. They sat on the banquette opposite us

in a little narrow restaurant having dinner. The man had a round, self-satisfied face with glasses on it. The woman was fadingly pretty

in a big hat. There was nothing conspicuous about them, nothing particularly noticeable.

it was the husband's birthday, and the wife had planned a little surprise for him. It arrived in

the form of a small but glossy birthday cake with one pink candle burning in the centre.

at him and you saw this, and you thought, oh, now, don't be like that. But he was like that.

question is very expensive. And I am also 24 years old. So, I don't know what a whole human is yet,

but I think that it for me a huge part of feeling whole in my life has to do

with the balance of giving and receiving.

temporary state and that the balance can tip, you know, either way, but the

moments where I feel the most whole and the more the most at peace is when I

feel like I've given and I've accepted

because I struggle a lot sometimes with both, you know, and I think we all do. And I think it's

very pretty to say, "Oh my god, I'm it's very difficult for me to receive. I'm so good at giving." I'm like, "If it's

difficult for you to receive, it's probably difficult for you to give at times as well, you know, and that's

that's something that I've experienced. But when I feel like yeah, like I was

52:19

able to show up for my community, for my family, for my friends, and I've also

52:25

been vulnerable and open enough to accept and receive what they wanted to

52:32

give me. I feel the best and I feel very whole,

52:39

incredibly fantastic. [laughter]

52:44

Thank you so much for your presence today, your very first podcast. It was so beautiful. Thank you for being

52:51

here. Thank you for being my teacher. Thank you for being somebody who is so dear to me. I really appreciate you.

Sistema 07 · Transcrições e Canais — ouça, leia, repita
